text_content:
That would be good if you got them to be able to breed under your care. Apparently they're under tremendous pressures from over collection and rainforest destruction in Madagascar. I am not suggesting that your frogs were victims of an animal crime ring or anything, like importation of endangered animals illegally... no judgement. It doesn't matter. What matters is they went somewhere good - you! ✌🏽

I just happened to see a documentary; children from villages go "herping", albeit not as a westerner would, lol. They catch the frogs (of various species) for money, along with chameleons & geckos. And, if they can do it without the mother noticing, baby lemurs. The reptiles & amphibians are to be thrown haphazardly into the child's bag, and this is after being (fairly violently) pursued and captured.  Chameleons aren't carefully coerced out of treetops, onto a pole to grab, like they do in Florida; no, the kids just knock 'em to the ground. Hm. You'd think theyd be in trouble for this from the ringleader right? I'd think they would be more careful knowing that their meager income is involved. Lol. And the kids sometimes "casually" swing the bags around as they skip home. Nice. But before home, a quick stop - go to the local animal dealer (aka importer) and he pays them one cent for each animal. I've never heard of more than one "shilling" or dollar being paid to these kids. Then the kids are paid and sent off; the reptiles are separated, and the lot of them are packed according to species, one on top of the other, and shipped internationally. It's really the worst for the chameleons, as they can't stand being social at all really. Let alone looked at haha. And they're just sensitive period. None of this was relating to your frogs by the way I was just appalled by it. Haha.

text_content: For any amphibian, don’t hold them. Their skin is extremely sensitive and absorbs anything it touches, including harmful oils, soaps, and chemicals from your hands. Handling them can dry out their skin, cause stress, and even lead to injury or illness. Many also breathe and stay hydrated through their skin, so human contact can interfere with these vital functions. While brief handling may be necessary at times, it should always be done with wet, clean hands or gloves and kept as short and gentle as possible.

video_title: Top 5 WORST Pet Frogs and 5 BETTER Options You've Never Heard Of!
video_description: Frogs make great pets! But what are the worst frogs for beginners and the better options that make for easier captive frogs?
text_content:
I've got several of these animals:

I own a breeding pair of Vietnamese Mossy frogs (Theloderma corticale). I don't yet have amazon milk frogs, but they are on my list. I find the mossy frogs to be generally quite cryptic, they stay hidden most of the time unless stressed or feeding. They don't seem to rely on their camouflage that much, preferring to stay in the water and just cling to plants and wood. They do go through more gregarious periods, but generally, their flightiness takes over. That being said, I also now have over 20 tadpoles from them with more on the way. They are prolific breeders.

White's tree frogs are great, but best if you have a red night light to watch them hunt when they are awake. I've got three and they are fantastic pets that require almost no maintenance outside of feeding and scrubbing shed skin off of the glass. Bioactive enclosures make it so the poop just sort of disappears mostly.

Spanish ribbed newts are awesome too. I own 10 of them in my breeding colony, and they are by far the easiest aquatic animal I've ever owned. Extremely hardy, very robust, "playful", amorous and inquisitive, often coming to the glass of the tank to investigate me when I walk in the room. Probably expecting food, but not always as those same inquisitive newts will sometimes ignore the food in favour of staring at me and following me around.

I've got a pixie frog, and while I agree that feeding one is certainly a fun thing to do, I'll also add that they are the only amphibian I've encountered that you can actually build an owner-pet relationship. Something nearly on par with a cat or dog. Indeed I think if you can figure out the right incentive and strategy, they might even be smart enough to be trained to do simple tricks (trying to come up with a method now, just to see). Mine has proven to be an avid problem solver (gradually weakening the lid on his tub of a course of a couple months till the screen came loose so he could escape), and discerning eater, often conveying displeasure when I try to feed him food other than superworms. He knows not to bite me, but treats others as dangerous or hostile. He willingly comes and sits in my presence when I give him free roaming time (which I highly recommend, so long as the floor or environment is free of toxic chemicals). I try not to read into things too much, but I am almost certain that the inner life of a Pixie is a tad more complex than a lot of other amphibians.

text_content: Most crested geckos in captivity are extremely overweight. When Blue River did the crested gecko documentary, and started research on the wild cresties in New Caledonia, they concluded captive bred geckos would benefit from a lower fat diet. They have tiny stomachs and don’t need much.

text_content: little trick to breeding any difficult species...look up the weather patterns for its country of origin, temp, humidity, rainy seasons, dry season, and breading times.  Hence if your frog lives in an arid area where its say dry for most of the year,, high temps, low humidity, and little rain, but in march every year they have flash floods, with months of rains and low temps, during this time is when they breed...you would want to mimic this in your tank, introduce male to female only when they would in nature cross paths and breed, follow husbandry of the particular species, do they mate for life, do they typically stay iin groups, or are they loners that only find each other to breed, etc..if you mimic this u will have far greater success in difficult breeds and breeds not typically in the domestic trade market. ..i learned this trick from a herpetologist that ran a reptile sanctuary in Ontario Canada  ..he had difficulty breeding his Burmese ..so he introduced a rainy season, which they typically in nature breed after..sure enough thats what hes been applying to his programs to all his projects with great success

text_content:
As a frog keeper, there's a problem I can identify: it seems the more engaging the frog is, the more downsides it has. For example, pixie frogs, pacman frogs, tomato frog and toads are quite easy as far as frogs go; fairly available, easy to house, inexpensive. On the other hand, these sorts of frogs are kinda boring. On the other end of the spectrum, dart frogs are incredibly wonderful to observe; absolutely beautiful, endearing and active. However, in addition to being particularly fragile, they are microphagists which is annoying. Tree frogs also have some liabilities, while also being very rewarding. I don't mind since I'm willing to put up with fruit flies and I still find joy in the more sedate frogs, but its these reasons why frogs in general aren't the best starter herps.

The solution therefor is arguably the tiger salamander. Housing them is easy, terrestrial salamander care is fairly similar to that of toads and other terrestrial frogs, they're big and colorful, and they have a very engaging personality, reputably begging for food and otherwise acting like leopard geckos. Except they aren't widely available captive bred...

video_description: Endangered Species Spotlight Anchicaya Harlequin Toad #endangeredspeciesspotlight #anchicayaharlequintoad #frog #ess ...
text_content: I genuinely appreciate the frog exposure, we need more of it. But it’s not accurate information. The frog you’re showing in the pictures is Oophaga histrionica, the “red head” locality. Also known as the harlequin poison frog, AKA a frog, not a toad. Not to be confused with harlequin toads, which are true toads and are from the “Atelopus” genus (which I personally work very closely with, specifically Atelopus barbotini). The animal you’re referring to is neither. You’re likely referring to Oophaga anchicayensis, also known as the Anchicaya poison frog. Which is not a toad, nor is it the animal shown of the pictures. Though being from the same genus (that being Oophaga), it’s very closely related to the frog shown on the pictures. Your information on the pet trade is also not completely accurate. Though unfortunately a lot of frogs are still illegally imported, there’s a lot of people working in the herpetoculture and vivarium hobby to minimise and prevent that where possible. Facilities like Tesoros de Colombia are working legally and responsibly with many poison dart frog and harlequin toad species, to both provide animals for the hobby and help populations in the wild, through release and conservation. Shipments are done properly. And the people in the hobby working with these animals know very well how to take care of them, unlike what you said in the video. Which can be proven, since hobbyists managed to breed many of these species way before zoos and conservationists even got close. Two notable examples being Dendrobates tinctorius and Atelopus barbotini (toads from the Atelopus genus specifically being incredibly hard to breed and everything needing to be exactly perfect for them to do so), the latter having not a single stable breeding population outside of the hobby, since many conservation facilities don’t work with them. Thus making the responsible and legal herpetoculture hobby extremely important for the future of these amazing animals. It’s unfortunate our hobby and conservation work gets put in such a bad light by animal rights extremists, would be nice to see some people actually doing the research themselves and spreading the amazing work and effort people in our hobby are putting into breeding and keeping these very delicate little animals, which great success (again, before zoos and conservationists ever succeeded or even tried). Hope you learned something. Keep up the passion for animals and conservation!! :)

text_content: Fruit walks are easy to keep as a colony. I recommend that the container be very tall and that the lid be of the variety that snaps down, not threaded because the larvae can get out of a threaded lid. Also, the taller the container, the easier it is to tap down the little crawlers to keep them from swarming out. Next, I recommend having a large Tupperware to tap some fruit walks out into  as a way to control how many come out when you shake it. Finally, don't let other fruit flies get into your culture. Keep them in your fridge and don't open them if you have fruit flies infesting your home. Why? The same reason Clint said not to mix the varieties of fruit walks. Because it refreshes the gene pool and makes it so that they fly again. Also, it doesn't even have to be specifically another variety of fruit fly. Mycovore gnats are close enough in relation that the species often interbreed. Mycovore gnats are also called beer flies or bar flies. They eat the yeast and mildew build-up around drains in human houses and food service businesses. So cover your drains and occasionally use the appropriate pest control to prevent getting infestations from your feeder colonies. Trust me, that's an embarrassing problem to have. We all have probably had cricket problems before. Fruit flies are just as annoying.

text_content:
Mr. Adam Sir.  I keep Dendrobates tinctorius 'Citronella'.  I love em.  Your video was spot on.  

When I made up my terrarium using spray foam and dry lock, I added some medicine pill bottles, the red/orange ones you get for prescriptions, and embedded them in the foam.  I cut off the back sides, so it was a hollow tube.  I embedded them in the foam. The frogs LOVE these as hides.  What is really nice, too, is that if you get them flush against the back/side of the terrarium, you can then view them in the hide from the side/back of the terrarium.  Gives a neat perspective.

I also added a couple of inches of a rock layer, with a substrate barrier in mine.  With the humidity that high, it is a good idea.

For the humidity, I just used a cheap party fogger that I found on Amazon for $40.  Put it on a timer, and it works great.

Your content is great. Not too sure about why you felt you had to mention that you shouldn't put dart frogs in your orifices, but whatever.  lol

Keep up the great work.

text_content: Awesome video! I would like to comment about the last option of frog. I’ve been a experienced hobbyist for about 8 years and also being a zoo keeper for the last 2 years I have to say yes Leuc’s, Tincs and auratus are great beginners frogs as well as some other species like epipedobates they are hardy they can withstand temperatures ranging from high 60’s to high 70’s. You can also mist them as much as you want and will be completely fine. But Terribilis on the other hand have specific parameters that need to be met. First thing’s first if you get into buying Terribilis as your first frog just know this is a intermediate level frog. Terribilis are prone to having rot foot when the enclosure stays to wet. This can lead to them losing their feet and potentially cause them to pass due to their immobility. They are also supposed to be kept as close to 75 as you can. This is also a big one, dusting your feeders with calcium is a huge must with these guys. They are probably one of the biggest if not the biggest dart frogs in the world and they need the calcium to steer away from MBD and it also need the calcium for their egg production since they lay more then 20 eggs at a time. I hope this helps, please give me feedback and hopefully I can answer more questions about dart frogs that you may have. 👍🏼

text_content:
Please do not get any more frogs until you know something about them. Firstly, a ten gallon is way to small for those species. A twenty gallon is okay for them but the bigger the better. Secondly, cohabing different species in such a small tank is careless. The blue one is often hard to cohab anyways so two different tanks for them is the best. Third, before feeding you should have some calcium and vitamin powder on the flies. Fourthly, you should not touch them with your bare hands. They have very sensitive skin and although they aren’t toxic to you, you can have stuff on your hands that can be harmful to them. Lastly, they like to climb so 
have corkbark or other things for 
them too climb on. Im not trying to ruin anything for you but please get some more knowledge about dart frogs so they actually can enjoy their home. Hope you take this into consideration if you ever want to get more frogs. Bonus tip, get a hygrometer so you always know when to water them.

video_title: I Got a Pet Poison Dart Frog (DEADLY!)
video_description: Apparently the worlds deadliest frog makes a great pet!! So I tested it and it was awesome! if I used any sounds or videos that are ...
text_content:
Next time when feeding, you need to coat the bugs with calcium D3 powder to prevent Metabolic Bone Disease which is caused from calcium deficiency, or a lack of calcium. The calcium is important for the well being of the bones, nerve and muscle functions, and without it, can cause deformities, and even death.

Please do not mix dart frog species or morphs. All frogs must be the same morph and species to prevent territorialism and aggression which can lead to both frogs attacking, and potentially harming each other. Different morphs or species being housed together can also cause them hybridize which may mess up genes.

For the substrate, use natural substrate like coco fiber or reptisoil. The substrate in this video is just pebbles which can be used for a drainage layer at the bottom for water, but not good for a top layer.

While you did add in leaf litter, a bit of moss, such as spagma moss on the ground to maintain humidity in the vivarium would be a good idea.

Try not to add too much ventilation. Yes, a little bit of ventilation is good, but giving too much can cause both the enclosure, and the frogs to dry out. You can try covering up the top screen a bit, and leave a small amount of ventilation.

Please do not handle your frogs. Me saying this is not me caring too much about these animals, but me giving advice. Holding your dart frogs wont cause a bond, but will cause stress to the frog. These animals are skittish, and will flee when feeling threatened. Attempting to chase and grab it will only cause stress. All amphibians absorb liquids like water through their skin, and handling your frog can transfer oils, salt, and substances on your hand to the frog which can be harmful, so please try to refrain frog holding your dart frog from now on.

video_title: How Hunters Used Poison Dart Frogs 😲
text_content:
For more information:

The Emberá Chocó and Noanamá Chocó tribes, situated on the Pacific slopes of the Andes in Colombia, are known for using poison dart frogs to tip their blowgun darts. They specifically utilize three species of poison dart frogs: Phyllobates aurotaenia, Phyllobates bicolor, and Phyllobates terribilis. 

To collect the poison, the frogs are roasted over a fire. However, the steroids present in P. terribilis are so potent that it is sufficient to simply rub the dart on the back of the frog without killing it.

video_title: Beginner Poison Dart Frogs - Species Overview and Selection Guide
video_description: Choosing your first dart frog species can be a very difficult task, but today's video will hopefully help you narrow down your choice.
text_content: For most beginners, I would recommend Dendrobates tinctorius as an ideal choice. They are bold, relatively hardy, and grow to an impressive size, plus they come in a range of beautiful locality-specific color morphs. Additionally, males of this species have a quieter call, which is a bonus. However, with proper research, any dart frog species can be suitable for beginners. Each species has its unique characteristics, much like people, making the world of dart frogs both diverse and fascinating.

text_content:
I started with 5 Banded Leucomelas, and they are a little more shy than I was hoping for, especially for the first year. I see them out and about a lot more now, probably ~50% of the time I look in on them, but rarely more than 2-3 at a time.

Then I got a pair of Blue Sipaliwini Tinc's and they're much bolder. In retrospect, I should have started with them. I got the Leuc's because I wanted a good group frog, but I think it's better starting out to have a pair of frogs you always see, than a group of frogs that hide all the time.

Variabilis Southern are next on my list, or maybe Varadero's.

video_title: Beginner Poison Dart Frogs - Species Overview and Selection Guide
video_description: Choosing your first dart frog species can be a very difficult task, but today's video will hopefully help you narrow down your choice.
text_content:
Excellent video. I keep a bunch of the ones you listed, Amizonica being my first. I now have Oyapock, Azureus, Vanzolinii, Serensis, Fantastica monte cristo, Epipedobates anthonyi, and Opphaga pumilio punta laurel. The boldest are definitely the Oyapock and Azureus, but the Anthonyi are also incredibly bold. The fantastica is by far the least bold, often I will go 6 or 8 weeks without seeing her. the Serensis is similar but I hear him every morning. when I first got into frog keeping I bought frogs based on their beautiful coloration, but after a few years of keeping I started to buy them more based on their calls. the Oophaga pumilio probably has my favorite call, but the Vanzolinii, Serensis, and Leucamelas have incredible calls too. I wake up daily to them. The Anthonyi call more than all the other frogs combined and also have a very beautiful call. 

I would agree with everything you said here. You just make me wish I could have some phyllobates, unfortunately the entire genus is prohibited where I live for some insane reason I can't understand. 

Interestingly I keep my anthonyi in a very unconventional way. They share a very large paludarium with an emerald tree boa. I am very aware that darts can and readily do drown so a paludarium is not typically suggested but these ones seem to do well in there. The babies have never known any other environment having come out of the water in that tank. The tree boa is fully nocturnal and fully arboreal where the species of darts are quite terrestrial and diurnal. The tank is 48"x48"x18" or 122x122x45 cm with about 11 gallons of water which houses some neon tetras.

video_title: Beginner Poison Dart Frogs - Species Overview and Selection Guide
video_description: Choosing your first dart frog species can be a very difficult task, but today's video will hopefully help you narrow down your choice.
text_content:
I wanted to add the advice for beginners not to start with froglets that are too young. Better to invest a little more money and get a proven pair, an adult group or stable subadults. Don't buy anything less than 6 months old as a rule of thumb. If you are buying froglets or subadult frogs, buy at least 4 to minimize your chance of ending up with zero females in the end. It can be surprisingly hard to get hold of a species, or locale, again in your area.
And if you are starting out with an adult pair, you could even start with Oophaga pumilio as your first species. Adults especially of the bigger locales are super hardy and robust, probably more so than most Ranitomeya species. They are bold, long lived, have an awesome call, and take care of their young. You probably shouldn't start with Dendrobates or Phyllobates if you are actually specifically into Oophaga.

I was so nervous, I left my 6 frogs alone for 10 days. I had a auto mister, but was so nervous about the food.

I fed them a bunch of flies the 2 days leading up to the trip and put a culture in right before leaving. I returned home and everyone was happy and healthy!

For anyone worrying, prep for it and they'll be just fine!

text_content: Another interesting fact about the golden and predominantly red ones and the orange ones is their main staple (food and poison source) are the nasty south american fire ants. The interesting bit is they lay a single egg in a pitcher plant (bromeliad etc.) which then catches flies and such for itself and the tadpole. When the tadpole starts to develope legs and grows more frog like but not a full fledged frog, the parent returns and deposits a unfertilized egg in the plant to ensure food for the final stage of developement. Truly amazing little creatures.

text_content:
For those confused.

Venomous animals inject their toxins, typically through bites or stings, while poisonous animals secrete or excrete toxins that are harmful when touched or ingested.
The most venomous is a Box Jellyfish

Edit for those saying the cone snail

Box jellyfish venom causes instant, severe pain and can lead to cardiac arrest and death within minutes, while a cone snail's venom causes paralysis over hours, potentially leading to respiratory and heart failure, but death is rarer and takes longer. The box jellyfish is considered the most venomous marine animal and is a direct threat to human life, whereas the geography cone snail's venom is also highly dangerous, but death is nearly exclusive to one species and is less common than from box jellyfish stings. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) considers The Box Jellyfish to be the most venomous marine animal in the world.

video_title: The Golden Dart Frog is CRAZY
text_content: The funny thing is that the "in the wild" statement is key. Because in the pet trade they eat fruit walks (wingless fruit flies), but since in the wild they get their toxins from the alkaline metals they get from the ants and other insects they eat, in captivity off of that diet, they have no poison.

text_content:
You missed the coolest part about them. How they are born. Where they are from, it either rains often. When these guys find a mate, they stay together and work. The mom will lay a single egg in a tiny puddle at the base of a plant that can hold water like a cup. Once they mate mom and dad go tree to tree, bush to bush. Anywhere water can accumulate. Mom lays a single fertilized egg in each spit. And then dad carefully remembers it. Its his job to check in the eggs place by place. Mom helps too. But mom also needs to eat more. 

Sometimes, the tiny puddles holding their single egg, will start to dry up. Their eggs will die, if left their tadpoles have hatched they are 100% reliable in being fully submerged in water. 

When the water gets low, dad or mom will put the egg in their mouth, and take it to a new puddle. 

Once they hatch, they need food. They also need protection.  Mom and dad cannot stay with them all day. They have hundreds of eggs to look after. So, how do they feed their tadpoles? Sometimes, bugs will land near by their puddle to drink, the tadpoles will eat them. If there is more than one tadpoles in a puddle, they will eat each other. But LUCKILY for them. They are poison dart frogs. 

To keep their tadpoles safe, mom goes to every puddle and lays a single unfertilized egg containing poison they will use to stay alive. Mom and dad don't need to stand watch. The wild life knows that those tadpoles are. As the tadpoles eat it mom's poisonous unfertilized eggs. They are now as poisonous as their mom and dad. Make them safe from everything. 

I don't know what CAN eat them. But this is nature, SOMETHING must eat them. Like can't go unchecked like that without burning it all down. But they way they do stuff is kind of amazing.  Mother nature is magical.

text_content: Heyya, dart frog breeder here, I work with 10 species currently. Just wanted to let you know that you are using the right supplements, but you are giving far too much vitamin A. Only breeding frogs really need it, and it's recommended only once a month for those breeding frogs unless they froglets are morphing out with spindly leg syndrome (sls.) Giving it once a week to non-breeding frogs can cause you a lot more problems than benefits. It is also very much frowned upon in the dart frog hobby to keep different species, even different morphs of the same species together in the same enclosure. I would get more plants in there it will help keep it more humid in there for them. As someone else has said, the enclosure looks really dry. Humidity should be between 70-100% at all times.

text_content: This is known as mutualism a type symbiotic relationship where both organisms benefit and none is harmed or hurt a plus plus for both (Learned from Marine science class)

video_title: Tarantulas Keep Frogs As Pets? 😮
text_content: These animals have better friendships than most people in the world

video_title: Tarantulas Keep Frogs As Pets? 😮
text_content: For those of you who are wondering. This phenomenon was discovered in the Amazon rainforest. These aren’t general frog and tarantulas but are specific species, dotted humming frog and Colombian Lesserblack Tarantula.

video_title: Tarantulas Keep Frogs As Pets? 😮
text_content: Well it’s called “Symbiosis” for a reason. Symbiosis is common in some parts of the animal kingdom. The frog benefits the tarantula because it feeds off of the parasites in its home and in return the tarantula protects it from predators. That’s symbiosis. They don’t have to like each other, because they need each other to survive.

video_title: Tarantulas Keep Frogs As Pets? 😮
text_content: This is actually called mutualism, it’s when two organisms in a relationship that both get benefits. It’s really interesting how animals live! 😊

text_content: Loved these different frogs but you need to check out the Australian Motorbike frog which is native and only found in my corner of Australia in the South West of Western Australia. Most amazing sound to listen too, especially from a frog!!

video_title: White's Tree Frog, The Best Pet Amphibian?
video_description: The White's tree frog, Australian green tree frog, or dumpy frog (Litoria caerulea) is one of the best pet amphibians that you can get ...
text_content: I had several of these guys as pets when I was a kid, and still have fond memories of them. One of them actually managed to escape the enclosure and we couldn't find him and thought he was dead, then later on my mom went to get the vacuum cleaner out of the hall closet and found him sitting right on it, covered in dust but still alive even after at least several days without water.

video_title: White's Tree Frog, The Best Pet Amphibian?
video_description: The White's tree frog, Australian green tree frog, or dumpy frog (Litoria caerulea) is one of the best pet amphibians that you can get ...
text_content: The whites tree frog is an arboreal species of amphibian (hence tree frog). It should be in a tank where it has more height rather than width as it will want to express its natural behaviour of climbing. You can keep it in a wider tank but make sure there is height as well, because this way the animal is most likely going to be less stressed.

video_title: White's Tree Frog, The Best Pet Amphibian?
video_description: The White's tree frog, Australian green tree frog, or dumpy frog (Litoria caerulea) is one of the best pet amphibians that you can get ...
text_content: The Whites tree frog is one of the best pets I've ever had.  Mine actually got out and was lost for several days.  I ended up finding him and he was pretty crusty but made a full recovery.  They button down and make a mucus layer to survive droughts so I think that saved him.  Also he clearly recognized me as his owner, whenever someone had him out and I was near he'd immediately jump to me.  Every now and again him and the chameleon would get love pinkies as a treat.  I always thought that was pretty neat.  Great pet, 100% recommended.

Omg, my dumpys are my best friends. Chubs, my boy, loves to climb on me, and he likes to be stroked between his eyes. (With wet, and meticulously clean hands of course), Karen, my smaller froggy isn't as social but she likes to eat worms from my hands. 
I also have a green Tree frog (Trevor) and a red eyed (Sage) and I almost never handle them. Trevor endures his check ups quite well, and stays still once I scoop him up, but I practically never touch Sage. They're also much less social, and climb away from me when I come near the enclosure. But I love all of my froggies dearly.

text_content:
Try to simulate their natural environment as far as you can. Including morning mist and rain. Africa is hot, but it also has the day-night cycle of moisture and heat coolness, which are important for the frogs.
These frogs also lay their eggs inside the soil and guard them, so avoid digging them up and touching them at all costs. Before breeding, try to create a bioactive container, so you can rest easy with the soils not going bad and enduring the moisture levels and the frogs (and their waste) themself better. Their skin is delicate, so the hard and sharp gravel in the tank is not the best possible choice. The noises the frogs make might be because of breeding. Frogs usually make noise to attract a mate.  Please keep on reporting how things are going!

video_description: Do Rain Frogs make good pets? No one can seem to agree, so let's find out. Enclosure build, feeding, tiny insects, and so much ...
text_content: They probably need deep soil that holds their burrows as stable as natural African soil would. A mother Rain Frog digs a 2-story burrow when she breeds. The deeper downstairs burrow is for the eggs and higher upstairs one is for mom to stay and keep an eye on things. When the babies have their legs and are ready to come out, the whole family leaves the burrow together. I would guess it's also important not to dig up the mom while she's looking after her babies. Being dug up all the time probably makes the females feel too insecure to breed. In the wild, they can hibernate for weeks waiting for rain.

text_content: For future reference, try to avoid swabbing frogs with dirt on them when you're taking swabs for disease testing. Dirt can inhibit the PCR reaction and result in a false negative. For best results, gently rinse your frogs off with distilled water before swabbing. You're trying to collect skin cells when you swab your frogs, so it's fine if you rinse them off first, just make sure to use gentle pressure when swabbing.

text_content: Although conservation efforts are important, I'm always bewildered by creatures that seem to refuse to survive.  There's creatures like rats, fruit flies, and Africanized ants that will survive getting shot, nuked, or asteroided.  But then there's creatures like pandas, pupfish, and rain frogs that seem to be trying their best to remove themselves from the food chain in every way possible.

text_content: Good luck with breeding them. I've yet to hear of any success stories. Most of the rain frog accounts I follow on Instagram are Japanese or Taiwanese because they are very common (and sold for very cheap) in those regions. I think the key to breeding them would require not disrupting their enclosure while imitating the rainy season. But I'm not sure how long you should have unchanged dirt in their  enclosure for risk of infection, especially if the moisture and water just stays there. idk if you can right a tank to allow the water to drip out from the bottom so too much moisture doesn't accumulate in the soil because you might need a lot of water for this experiment. I'm just very impressed that you used imitating the rainy season for feeding the fussy one. You might need to resort to that for the other ones for the breeding experiment. I pray for your success because I wish these things were way more common in the states and not just wild catches.

text_content:
I used to work at a chytrid research lab.  (Almost caused a leak once 😅)

You’d be surprised how much the disease has spread.  Where I live a lot of the native leopard and red legged frogs are getting pushed out of their natural habitat by bullfrogs, which spread the disease but are more resistant to it.  

Whenever you get salamanders or frogs keep them isolated until test results come back and do an inspection for light brown mottling on their sides, mottling or redness on the stomach, and a damage around the mouth. Pet distributors are one of the biggest ways Chytrid (Ki-trid) can spread.

text_content: Having specialists keep animals for pets actually can help figure out why certain populations might be struggling and how to help them (emphasis on specialists). The figuring out how to simulate when they know to feed is genius and things like someone down further suggesting breeding maybe has been unsuccessful because they lay eggs deeper than normal burrows can be clues as well for where to look if something is going on (for example if a population doesn't seem to be laying eggs). It's a lot harder than people think to just observe them in the wild... there's so much we don't even know about animals that are everywhere (like crows and all their practices as an example) and if you can simulate their environment you understand their environment. I definitely don't think your average person should keep these as pets but this is a fascinating attempt to figure these little guys out. There's also the fact that if you can breed it you can save it.

video_title: My dart frog rain forest vivarium: full tour!!!
text_content:
Well done! Transitioning from aquariums to terrestrial or vice versa can always be daunting but you have a great setup. I don’t even bother with many ficus species as they are too fast growing and take over terrariums quickly. Sometimes slower growing plants can be very rewarding but I also totally understand why keepers use species like ficus pumila.

The more you culture flies the better you’ll get at knowing when to seed a new culture. One to feed, one to breed, and one to seed. Also I’ve had success using a thin bead of silicone between two glass panes as if you were sealing them. After it cures I run a razor through it and you’re left with something that is much closer to being sealed. May not be advisable to do when frogs are in there as the fumes could be harmful, but the silicone cures rather quickly. Just an idea

text_content:
Well done, BBC Earth 'science'. "Poison arrow frog" is a generic and historical term for around 180 to 200 different species, spread across 12 or more genera, of poison frog,  and native to the tropical rain forests of Central and South America. Science, rather more generally, refers to all of these frogs as "poison dart frogs" because out of the over 180 species, only three or four of the highly specific phyllobates genus have ever been documented as being used to poison hunting _arrows_. The frogs in this video are not poison arrow frogs, they are poison dart frogs.

So much for 'science'.

Fail.

PS: Single quotes indicate a pejorative.

You forgot to mention about the Siberian Silver Foxes.
It was part of a Soviet Experiment to recreate Canine Domestication in decades rather than centuries, by using selective breeding methods, using only the most tamed and friendly ones, and it changed their own biology and reproductive cycle.
For anyone who wants to know more, look up "The Siberian Silver Fox Experiment", and look for the one with a bunch of Red Foxes walking through the street.

text_content:
Five Nights at Freddy's (FNaF) is a massive and intricate horror game franchise known for its deep, often cryptic lore. It primarily revolves around a fictional restaurant chain called Freddy Fazbear's Pizza, reminiscent of Chuck E. Cheese, where animatronic characters entertain children by day and become terrifying, haunted threats by night.
​Here's a breakdown of the core elements of the FNaF lore:
​1. The Missing Children Incident and the Killer:
​William Afton: The primary antagonist and co-founder of Fazbear Entertainment. He is a serial killer who, motivated by a dark obsession with immortality and remnant (the supernatural energy left behind by tragic events), murders several children.
​The Missing Children Incident: This refers to the initial murders where William, often disguised as the animatronic Spring Bonnie, lures and kills at least five children at Freddy Fazbear's Pizza.
​Possession: The spirits of these murdered children possess the animatronics:
​Freddy Fazbear
​Bonnie the Bunny
​Chica the Chicken
​Foxy the Pirate Fox
​Golden Freddy (or Fredbear), often believed to be possessed by the spirit of his first victim, Cassidy, or a combination of spirits.
​2. The Afton Family Tragedy:
​Elizabeth Afton: William's daughter, who is killed by his creation, Circus Baby, at Circus Baby's Entertainment and Rental (a sister location). Her spirit possesses Baby.
​The Crying Child (Evan Afton/BV): William's younger son, who is terrified of the animatronics. He dies from a bite inflicted by Fredbear (the original Golden Freddy animatronic) during a birthday party prank orchestrated by his older brother, Michael. This is known as "The Bite of '83." His spirit is often theorized to be Golden Freddy, or part of Golden Freddy, or even Puppet.
​Michael Afton: William's older son. He attempts to atone for his father's sins and his own role in his brother's death. He works at various Fazbear locations, including the first Freddy's, Circus Baby's Entertainment and Rental (where he is scooped and becomes a walking corpse inhabited by Ennard), and eventually becomes the night guard in later games, trying to free the trapped souls. He is a significant protagonist.
​3. Remnant and Immortality:
​William Afton discovers "remnant," a supernatural metallic substance created by agony and traumatic death. He learns that by infusing animatronics with remnant, he can imbue them with consciousness and effectively make the souls "immortal" within the machines.
​His experiments with remnant and his desire to understand and control it are key to his villainy and the prolonged suffering of the children's souls.
​4. Key Locations and Events:
​Freddy Fazbear's Pizza (Various Incarnations): The primary setting for many of the games, where the haunted animatronics lurk.
​Circus Baby's Entertainment and Rental: A more advanced animatronic facility designed by William Afton, featuring "funtime" animatronics designed to capture children.
​Fazbear's Fright: The Horror Attraction: A haunted attraction built around the urban legends of Freddy Fazbear's Pizza, where Springtrap (William Afton trapped in the Spring Bonnie suit) is discovered.
​Freddy Fazbear's Pizzeria Simulator (FNaF 6): Michael Afton, guided by Henry Emily, creates a new pizzeria as a trap to lure and burn all remaining animatronics containing remnant (Springtrap, Molten Freddy, Scrap Baby, Lefty) to finally free the souls.
​Ultimate Custom Night: Often interpreted as William Afton's personal hell, tormented by the animatronics he created and the souls he wronged.
​Help Wanted: A VR game within the FNaF universe, where a digital virus (Glitchtrap, an AI manifestation of William Afton's consciousness) tries to escape into the real world.
​Security Breach: Set in the massive Mega Pizzaplex, featuring new animatronics and the return of William Afton (as Burntrap) and other older threats.
​5. Henry Emily:
​William Afton's business partner and co-founder of Fazbear Entertainment.
​He is the brilliant engineer behind many of the animatronics.
​His daughter, Charlotte (Charlie), is William's first victim, her spirit possessing The Puppet.
​Henry becomes the force for good, dedicating his life to ending William's terror and freeing the souls, culminating in the "FNaF 6" fire.
​6. The Puppet (Marionette):
​A highly significant animatronic, often seen as a protector.
​Possessed by Henry's daughter, Charlotte.
​It's believed to be the one who gave life to the other animatronics by placing the spirits of the murdered children into them.
​7. Springtrap/Scraptrap/Burntrap:
​William Afton's ultimate fate: after dismantling the original animatronics and releasing the spirits, he is cornered by the freed souls and crushed inside the Spring Bonnie suit, becoming Springtrap.
​He survives fires and appears in multiple games, continuously trying to return.
​Summary of the Narrative Arc:
​The FNaF lore tells a tragic story of a family torn apart by a monstrous father, the enduring suffering of murdered children, and the desperate attempts of others (Michael, Henry) to bring an end to the cycle of violence and free the tormented souls. It's a complex puzzle of unreliable narrators, hidden clues, and evolving mysteries that has kept fans speculating for years.

video_title: What Is The Prisoners Dilemma 😬
text_content:
This logic isn’t working in real life, only work in a game

In order for this too work for prisoners, this rule must have supported by Law

If we look at the Prisoner’s Dilemma realistically, the Warden is just another player who can’t be trusted. Their offer only exists because they’re actually the ones in trouble—if they had enough evidence, they wouldn't be asking for a confession in the first place. When nobody trusts anybody, a deal from the authority is just a trap, and the Warden is likely to betray both prisoners anyway. In this situation, the confession is the only leverage the prisoners have. Staying silent isn't just about being a good partner; it’s a strategic move to keep the upper hand and avoid getting screwed over by a system that has every reason to lie to you. Most people miss this because they're too busy worrying about their partner, forgetting that the person holding the keys is the one with the biggest reason to cheat.
